title: Avoiding Lookahead Bias: The Data Leak
concept: Lookahead bias is when future information leaks into the training data (like using tomorrow's price to predict today's), which ruins model reliability.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Which feature would you use to track the all-time maximum temperature recorded up to today?
exercises.explain: Correct! An expanding window maximum tracks the cumulative highest value from the very beginning of the series.

cards.title: Reading the cheat sheet
cards.body: Imagine taking a history test with the answer sheet sitting right next to you. You score 100%! But when you are tested in the real world without the cheat sheet, you fail. In time series forecasting, if you accidentally let future information slip into your model's training features, it will 'cheat' and look amazing in training, but fail completely in real life.

cards.title: What is Lookahead Bias?
cards.body: Lookahead bias (or data leakage) occurs when information from the future is used to create features for predicting the past or present. For example, using tomorrow's stock price (t+1) as a feature to predict today's price (t) is a lookahead leak.

cards.title: A time machine error
cards.body: Imagine you are building a model to predict who wins a horse race. If your model's features include 'tomorrow's newspaper sports section', you are using a time machine. In the real world, you don't have tomorrow's newspaper yet, so your model won't work.

cards.title: The average leak
cards.body: Suppose you want to predict daily sales. You create a feature: 'the average daily sales of the entire year'. If you train your model on January using this feature, the model is using information from February through December (the future!) to predict January. This is a severe leak.

cards.title: Shuffling breaks time series
cards.body: In standard machine learning, we randomly shuffle data before splitting it into training and testing sets. But in time series, shuffling is a disaster! Shuffling mixes future rows with past rows, allowing future data to leak into the training set. You must always split data chronologically.

cards.title: Chronological Splitting
cards.body: How to split time series data correctly to avoid lookahead bias:

left.text:
Shuffles rows randomly.
Training set contains days 1, 3, 5, 8.
Testing set contains days 2, 4, 6.
(Future leaks into past!)

right.text:
Splits at a single point in time.
Training: Days 1 to 80.
Testing: Days 81 to 100.
(Past predicts the future!)

exercises.prompt: Which of these features introduces lookahead bias when predicting today's weather?

options.text: The average temperature of the past 7 days.

exercises.explain: Correct! Using tomorrow's actual temperature requires future information that would not be available in real-world forecasting.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Shuffling a time series dataset randomly before splitting it into training and testing sets prevents lookahead bias.
exercises.explain: False! Random shuffling mixes future data points into the training set, causing severe lookahead bias and data leakage.

exercises.sentence: To avoid lookahead bias, we must split our training and testing data ___ along the timeline.
exercises.explain: A chronological split ensures that the model is only trained on past data and evaluated on future data.

exercises.prompt: Which of these describes a safe time series split that avoids lookahead bias?

options.text: Train on January through October, and test on November and December.
Randomly select 80% of days for training and 20% for testing.
Use the average of all days in the year as a feature in the training set.

exercises.explain: Correct! Splitting chronologically at a specific date ensures no future data leaks into the training period.

exercises.prompt: Your time series model got 99.9% accuracy during offline testing, but performs terribly when deployed to predict tomorrow's stock market. What is the most likely cause of this discrepancy?

options.text: Lookahead bias leaked future information (like future price trends) into the offline training features, which isn't available in production.
The model was trained for too many days.
The stock market did not use a rolling window.

exercises.explain: Exactly! High training performance but terrible real-world performance is a classic symptom of lookahead bias. The model 'cheated' by looking ahead during testing, but in production, the future has not happened yet! You've completed the unit on Time Series Features!
